edges.evidence: Both papers study trust formation on Airbnb, and the 2020 study broadens the earlier focus on personal photos to a wider set of online trust-building mechanisms from the provider perspective.
The later paper examines how trust shapes intention to use Airbnb in ASEAN countries, building on earlier evidence that profile-based cues affect trust on the platform.
Both papers analyze how user-facing social cues on Airbnb influence decisions, with the 2023 study extending cue-based profile research to racial-discrimination reduction via endorsements.
As a systematic review of the sharing economy, the 2025 paper synthesizes foundational empirical Airbnb studies including early work on trust and reputation mechanisms.
The 2025 review consolidates prior sharing-economy evidence on trust, including provider-side analyses of trust-building mechanisms on Airbnb.
The systematic review incorporates earlier Airbnb research on consumer loyalty as part of the broader sharing-economy knowledge base.
The questionnaire paper develops a measure of workers' experiences of algorithmic management, operationalizing constructs previously examined in relation to workplace well-being.
The scale-development study draws on the app-worker perspective literature to formalize and measure how workers experience algorithmic HRM control.
The food-delivery worker study extends app-worker research on algorithmic control by focusing specifically on messaging apps as infrastructures for learning, resistance, and solidarity.
Both papers examine how platform work reshapes workers' lives beyond immediate tasks, and the 2024 article deepens this line by tracing trajectories of exploitation and insecurity.
The 2025 annual review synthesizes organizational research on algorithmic management, including prior evidence about its effects on worker well-being.
The review integrates HRM and organizational scholarship on algorithmic management, explicitly encompassing worker-centered studies of app-based control.
Because it reviews the maturation of algorithmic-management research, the 2025 paper builds on new measurement work such as the algorithmic management questionnaire.
The 2025 review extends broader new-economy work design discussions by centering algorithmic management as a core organizational phenomenon rather than a peripheral case.
The Ant Forest study provides empirical evidence on gamified environmental behavior change, directly advancing the research agenda outlined in the earlier climate-engagement review.

notes: Most defensible new links cluster in two areas: Airbnb/sharing-economy trust research and algorithmic-management/gig-work research, with one clear gamification-climate edge; I found no strong contradiction pairs from the provided metadata, so none were added.
